topic: A person finds out they were adopted through a DNA test
raw_output:
[story 1]

The printout lay starkly on the kitchen counter, the fluorescent light reflecting off the damning percentages. Elias stared at the screen, his breath catching in his throat. Every line of the report seemed to vibrate with a silent accusation. He traced the names—names he’d always accepted as his own—and suddenly, the foundation of his life felt like shifting sand. His hands, which had been steady moments before, began to tremble, causing the paper to flutter slightly. He pushed back from the counter, the chair scraping loudly against the tile floor, and stumbled toward the window, needing the cold, indifferent expanse of the morning sky to offer some kind of solid ground.

[story 2]

"This is… impossible," Sarah whispered, the words catching on a dry swallow. She kept re-reading the genetic markers, her eyes darting from the data to the framed photograph on the shelf—a picture of a smiling woman she barely recognized. A strange, hollow ache settled deep in her chest, a pressure building behind her sternum. She gripped the edge of the desk so tightly her knuckles bleached white. When the final confirmation appeared, a slow, deliberate movement took over her. She didn't cry; instead, she simply slid the paper into a drawer, the sound of the latch clicking echoing in the sudden, profound silence of the room.

[story 3]

Mark sat rigid on the edge of the sofa, the phone still warm in his palm. He kept wiping his sweaty palms onto his jeans, a repetitive, anxious motion. The information had landed like a physical blow, knocking the air from his lungs. He looked around the living room, at the familiar furniture, the comforting clutter, and saw none of it. Everything seemed suddenly overlaid with a strange, unwelcome distance. He stood up abruptly, pacing the length of the rug three times before stopping, his gaze fixed on a spot on the wall as if trying to memorize the texture of the plaster, trying desperately to anchor himself against the swirling disorientation inside.

Every line of the report seemed to vibrate with a silent accusation. He traced the names—names he’d always accepted as his own—and suddenly, the foundation of his life felt like shifting sand. She kept re-reading the genetic markers, her eyes darting from the data to the framed photograph on the shelf—a picture of a smiling woman she barely recognized. A strange, hollow ache settled deep in her chest, a pressure building behind her sternum. She gripped the edge of the desk so tightly her knuckles bleached white. When the final confirmation appeared, a slow, deliberate movement took over her. She didn't cry; instead, she simply slid the paper into a drawer, the sound of the latch clicking echoing in the sudden, profound silence of the room.

The information had landed like a physical blow, knocking the air from his lungs. He looked around the living room, at the familiar furniture, the comforting clutter, and saw none of it.
